summary: New Orleans has always been a vibrant city with a unique political and social atmosphere. I recall how the French Quarter remains a year-round residential community as well as a tourist attraction, keeping the downtown business district lively. Despite many cities facing urban flight, New Orleans has maintained a viable inner-city population. It’s a place where local politics and community engagement have deep roots, and I’ve been involved in political organizing here since the late 1930s.

Louisiana politics have always been complex and multi-factional, especially in the late 1930s and 1940s when I was active with the People's League. We worked hard to bring integrity to government and to elect good leaders like Sam H. Jones in the 1940 gubernatorial election. The state has seen significant changes over time, including more citizen participation and shifts in political dynamics, especially with the rise of suburban areas and changes in racial integration. Throughout, Louisiana has produced remarkable congressional leaders who have shaped both state and national politics.

The People's League was a non-factional political organization formed around 1938 or 1939, with Hale Boggs as its leader. We were a group of young people in Louisiana who sought to restore integrity in government and fight corruption, particularly through legal actions and public rallies like those on the White House steps. Our efforts helped bring about federal prosecutions and influenced the 1940 Louisiana gubernatorial election, where we supported Sam H. Jones. After achieving our goals, members of the League integrated into other political factions but maintained a shared commitment to good governance.

Hale Boggs was the leader of the People's League and a central figure in Louisiana politics starting in the late 1930s. He graduated from law school in 1937 and quickly became involved in efforts to bring federal attention to corruption in Louisiana. Hale was a courageous politician who took stands on civil rights and open housing, even when it risked his political survival. His leadership helped guide others in the South toward more progressive positions, and he was respected for his integrity and ability to navigate complex political landscapes.

Lawrence Eustis was one of the members of the People's League during its formative years in the late 1930s. He was a state senator and part of the group of young people committed to reforming Louisiana politics and promoting integrity in government. Alongside others like Hale Boggs and Chuck Morrison, Eustis contributed to the League’s efforts to influence state politics and support candidates like Sam H. Jones in the 1940 gubernatorial election. His involvement exemplified the collaborative spirit of that reform movement.

Chuck Morrison was a key member of the People's League and later became mayor and ambassador to the Organization of American States. Along with his brother Jake, he was part of the young reform-minded group in Louisiana in the late 1930s that sought to bring about political change and integrity. His career trajectory from local politics to international diplomacy reflects the broad impact that the People's League members had. I remember how effective their work was in shaping Louisiana’s political landscape during that era.
cues: ambassador to the Organization of American States

summary: The Organization of American States came into my awareness through Chuck Morrison, who served as an ambassador there after his time as mayor. This international organization represented a broader stage where some Louisiana politicians, like Morrison, extended their influence beyond state and national politics. It symbolized the connection between local political leaders and international diplomacy during the mid-20th century. While my direct involvement was limited, it was notable how members of the People's League moved into such roles.

Raymond Monroe was an active political organizer in Louisiana who, although never holding elected office, played a significant role in political campaigns and organizations from the late 1930s onward. He was part of the People's League and contributed to the efforts to reform state politics and support candidates like Sam H. Jones. Monroe’s work behind the scenes was crucial in sustaining political momentum and organizing grassroots efforts throughout the ensuing gubernatorial races.

People's Week was an event associated with the early activities of the People's League in the late 1930s. It was a time when a group of young reformers, including Hale Boggs, organized to bring federal attention to corruption in Louisiana politics. The event helped galvanize support for legal actions and public demonstrations, such as rallies on the White House steps. People's Week symbolized the beginning of a concerted effort to restore integrity to government and engage citizens in political reform.

The United States Justice Department played a critical role in the late 1930s when the People's League sought federal intervention to address corruption in Louisiana. We worked closely with Justice officials to push for prosecutions that local authorities were reluctant to pursue. This cooperation was essential in bringing about grand jury investigations and legal actions that helped restore some measure of integrity to state government. The Justice Department’s involvement gave our efforts legitimacy and momentum during that period.

The United States Post Office was one of the federal agencies we engaged with during the People's League campaign against corruption in Louisiana in the late 1930s. Alongside the Justice Department, the Post Office had jurisdiction over certain aspects of federal law enforcement, and we sought their cooperation to ensure thorough investigations. Their involvement helped bring pressure on local officials who were not adequately pursuing justice. It was part of a broader strategy to use federal resources to combat entrenched political corruption.

The White House steps became a symbolic location for rallies organized by the People's League in the late 1930s. We held large public demonstrations there to draw national attention to the need for federal action against corruption in Louisiana. These rallies helped galvanize support and put pressure on federal authorities to act. The White House was a focal point for our efforts to show that ordinary citizens demanded integrity and justice from their government.

The 1940 Louisiana gubernatorial election was a pivotal moment for the People's League and our efforts to reform state politics. Starting in the fall of 1939, we campaigned actively to elect Sam H. Jones, who represented our ideals of integrity and good governance. We organized at the ward and precinct levels, focusing on voter registration and poll watching to ensure a fair election. Jones’s victory was a significant achievement that validated our work and set a new tone for Louisiana politics.

Sam H. Jones was the candidate of the People's League in the 1940 Louisiana gubernatorial election, and we worked hard to elect him. He came into office representing a break from the old political factions and a commitment to reform and integrity. Although he became more conservative over time, his election was a major success for our group and helped establish a foundation for more responsible government. Supporting Sam Jones involved grassroots organizing and a strong focus on voter participation.

World War II had a dispersing effect on many members of the People's League and the political reform movement in Louisiana. After the war, the original group did not come back together as a formal organization, but many of us maintained similar political attitudes and approaches. The war interrupted political careers and shifted priorities, but the lessons we learned about grassroots organizing and integrity in government stayed with us. It was a defining period that changed the political landscape in many ways.

The New Orleans French Quarter is not just a tourist attraction but also a year-round residential community that contributes to the vitality of the city. It supports the downtown business district, which remains lively and active. Unlike many cities where the inner city has been depleted, the French Quarter helps maintain a stable urban population in New Orleans. This unique aspect of the city has influenced local politics and community life throughout my experience here.

Neal Peirce is an author who has documented political developments, including an interview with Hale Boggs that I saw in the proofs of his new book. His work highlights the changing political landscape, especially regarding civil rights and the courage it took for politicians like Hale to take progressive stands. Peirce’s writing helps capture the significance of these moments and the personal and political risks involved. His insights provide valuable context for understanding the evolution of Southern politics.

The Open Housing Act was a significant piece of legislation that affected my husband, Hale Boggs, politically. His vote in favor of the Act almost cost him his political survival, reflecting the contentious nature of civil rights issues at the time. Despite the risks, Hale’s support showed his commitment to progress and helped pave the way for greater acceptance of civil rights measures in the South. The experience gave him a new sense of freedom to act on his convictions, even when it was politically dangerous.

Congressman Dave Treen and I have a very good working relationship. He is kind, polite, and gracious, and he has actively participated in testimonials held in my honor. We have collaborated on several projects, particularly those related to coastal issues in our districts. Despite political differences, we cooperate well and share a commitment to serving our constituents effectively.

NASA has been a focus of cooperation among members of the Louisiana congressional delegation, including myself. We have worked together on projects, such as a sixteen million dollar contract, to support NASA’s activities in our state. These joint efforts show how federal agencies and local representatives can collaborate for mutual benefit. NASA’s presence in Louisiana has been an important part of our district’s development and political agenda.

The Mississippi River is a critical geographic and economic feature for Louisiana, and it has been central to many political and environmental concerns. Last year, flooding was a major issue, and this year the water levels are even higher, prompting urgent meetings with the Corps of Engineers and other officials. Our delegation has worked together to address flood control and reassess flood insurance rates based on updated data. The river’s management affects many communities and requires coordinated political action.

entity: United States Army Corps of Engineers
summary: The United States Army Corps of Engineers has been a key partner in addressing flood control issues along the Mississippi River in Louisiana. We recently held meetings with them to discuss concerns about rising water levels and the effectiveness of levees. Their reevaluation of data on which flood insurance rates are based has been crucial in protecting our constituents. The Corps’ work directly impacts the safety and economic stability of many districts in our state.

The Democratic Party has historically been the dominant political force in Louisiana, especially during the mid-20th century when I was active politically. For many years, seats were considered safe within the party, but that has changed significantly in recent decades. The party has had to adapt to new political realities, including increased competition and shifting voter demographics. My husband, Hale Boggs, was a prominent Democrat who helped navigate these changes while advocating for civil rights and reform.

My husband, Hale Boggs, served in the United States House of Representatives, where he was known for his leadership and progressive stances on civil rights. The Louisiana delegation to the House has worked cooperatively on many issues, including joint letters and meetings to address district concerns. Our work in the House has involved collaboration on projects like NASA contracts and flood control efforts. The House has been a critical arena for advancing the interests of Louisiana and the South more broadly.

While my primary experience has been with the House delegation, the United States Senate members from Louisiana have occasionally met with us to coordinate on state issues. These meetings tend to be loosely structured but effective, especially when addressing large-scale concerns like flood control and federal contracts. Cooperation between House and Senate members has helped ensure that Louisiana’s interests are well represented at the federal level. This collaboration has been important for tackling complex problems that cross legislative boundaries.
